graph_vector_fulltext.message:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to answer the full “trace” (especially the exact trigger mechanics for reclassification and the detailed obligation set), because the context excerpt only states high-level points about (i) migration/asylum/border uses being treated as high-risk, (ii) EU database registration (including substantial modifications), and (iii) compliance with certain procedural requirements and international obligations.

Based strictly on the context, the regulatory consequences that are supported are:

- What triggers reclassification (supported by context):
  - The change in intended use: using an AI system “in the fields of migration, asylum and border control management” (e.g., to “recognis[e]… natural persons in the context of asylum management” / determine “eligibility… applying for a status”) is described as appropriate to classify as “high-risk, insofar as their use is permitted under relevant Union and national law.”
  - So, deploying the system to classify asylum applications is the key fact that would trigger treating it as high-risk (assuming this use falls within the migration/asylum management high-risk use cases set out in the annex referenced by the excerpt).

- What new obligations arise (supported by context):
  - EU database registration obligations apply to “high-risk AI systems,” and “any substantial modification of high-risk AI systems should also be registered in the EU database.”
  - For high-risk AI systems “in the area of … migration, asylum and border control management,” registration must be fulfilled “in a secure non-public section of the EU database,” with access “strictly” limited (the excerpt indicates limited access including the European Commission and market surveillance authorities).
  - The system “should comply with the relevant procedural requirements” set by Regulation (EC) No 810/2009 (Community Code on Visas) and Directive 2013/32/EU (asylum procedures), and “other relevant Union law.”
  - The use “should, in no circumstances, be used… as a means to circumvent… international obligations” under the UN Refugee Convention.

- Role of the original provider (only partially supported by context):
  - The context clearly says “providers of high-risk AI systems MUST_REGISTER in [the] EU database,” so if the system is now considered high-risk for this asylum use, the “provider” of that high-risk system has a registration obligation.
  - However, the context provided does not contain the rules that determine when the *deployer* becomes the “provider” due to a change of intended purpose. So I can’t state from this excerpt whether the original provider remains responsible as “provider,” or whether the deployer would be deemed the provider for the repurposed asylum-use system (and thus carry provider-side obligations).

If you paste the specific AI Act provisions you’re using for (a) “change of intended purpose” / when a deployer becomes a provider, and (b) the listed obligations for high-risk systems (or the relevant annex entry for migration/asylum), I can produce a complete step-by-step trace (trigger → reclassification → provider/deployer obligations → database section/publicity → procedural-law constraints).
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: An AI system classified as low-risk is deployed for general office document management. A deployer subsequently uses it to classify asylum applications. Trace the regulatory consequences: What triggers reclassification, what new obligations arise, and what is the role of the original provider?

  permits and associated complaints with regard to the objective to establish the eligibility of the natural persons  applying for a status, for the purpose of detecting, recognising or identifying natural persons in the context of  migration, asylum and border control management, with the exception of verification of travel documents. AI  systems in the area of migration, asylum and border control management covered by this Regulation should comply  with the relevant procedural requirements set by the Regulation (EC) No 810/2009 of the European Parliament
----
 case of high-risk AI  systems, set out in an annex to this Regulation, to which the high-risk AI systems correspond. Any substantial  modification of high-risk AI systems should also be registered in the EU database. For high-risk AI systems in the  area of law enforcement, migration, asylum and border control management, the registration obligations should be  fulfilled in a secure non-public section of the EU database. Access to the secure non-public section should be strictly 
----
of the Council (32), the Directive 2013/32/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council (33), and other relevant  Union law. The use of AI systems in migration, asylum and border control management should, in no circumstances,  be used by Member States or Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies as a means to circumvent their  international obligations under the UN Convention relating to the Status of Refugees done at Geneva on 28 July  1951 as amended by the Protocol of 31 January
----
 for the fundamental rights of the affected persons, in particular their  rights to free movement, non-discrimination, protection of private life and personal data, international protection  and good administration. It is therefore appropriate to classify as high-risk, insofar as their use is permitted under  relevant Union and national law, AI systems intended to be used by or on behalf of competent public authorities or  by Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies charged with tasks in the fields of migration, asylum and border

chunks.text: permits and associated complaints with regard to the objective to establish the eligibility of the natural persons applying for a status, for the purpose of detecting, recognising or identifying natural persons in the context of migration, asylum and border control management, with the exception of verification of travel documents. AI systems in the area of migration, asylum and border control management covered by this Regulation should comply with the relevant procedural requirements set by the Regulation (EC) No 810/2009 of the European Parliament

case of high-risk AI systems, set out in an annex to this Regulation, to which the high-risk AI systems correspond. Any substantial modification of high-risk AI systems should also be registered in the EU database. For high-risk AI systems in the area of law enforcement, migration, asylum and border control management, the registration obligations should be fulfilled in a secure non-public section of the EU database. Access to the secure non-public section should be strictly

of the Council (32), the Directive 2013/32/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council (33), and other relevant Union law. The use of AI systems in migration, asylum and border control management should, in no circumstances, be used by Member States or Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies as a means to circumvent their international obligations under the UN Convention relating to the Status of Refugees done at Geneva on 28 July 1951 as amended by the Protocol of 31 January

for the fundamental rights of the affected persons, in particular their rights to free movement, non-discrimination, protection of private life and personal data, international protection and good administration. It is therefore appropriate to classify as high-risk, insofar as their use is permitted under relevant Union and national law, AI systems intended to be used by or on behalf of competent public authorities or by Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies charged with tasks in the fields of migration, asylum and border
